category_summary:
A wave of major product launches dominated AI social media. **OpenAI** [released **GPT-5.4 mini**](/?date=2026-03-18&category=social#item-8428d8366bd7) and **nano**, new model tiers optimized for agents, coding, and computer use at 2x speed. **Greg Brockman** also [announced subagent support](/?date=2026-03-18&category=social#item-f78b3e8b9f91) in **Codex**, enabling parallelized autonomous workflows. **Sam Altman** [reflected on the end](/?date=2026-03-18&category=social#item-80b9444505a3) of character-by-character coding, drawing 3.7M views.

- **Midjourney** [launched **V8** in alpha](/?date=2026-03-18&category=social#item-850e94bed685) testing — 5x faster with native 2K output, improved text rendering, and new creative modes — sparking extensive community discussion
- **Tri Dao** [released **Mamba-3**](/?date=2026-03-18&category=social#item-c43237aa8efa), the most powerful linear model to date, noting the frontier has shifted to hybrid architectures across **Qwen**, **Kimi**, and **NVIDIA**
- **Anthropic's** Claude Skills guide [went mega-viral](/?date=2026-03-18&category=social#item-d2d00da49452) (2.9M views), with detailed community engagement on reusable prompt patterns and workflow transformation
- **LangChain** [shipped a fully OSS coding agent](/?date=2026-03-18&category=social#item-c3566f8f72b6), noting major engineering orgs like **Stripe** and **Coinbase** are building similar internal tools
- **Perplexity** [launched **Comet Enterprise**](/?date=2026-03-18&category=social#item-e39da1405bb6) with MDM deployment and **CrowdStrike** integration, while **Mistral** [announced **Forge**](/?date=2026-03-18&category=social#item-de8b552774ce) for enterprise model building and [released **Mistral Small 4**](/?date=2026-03-18&category=social#item-303da5601e71) (119B MoE, day-0 **vLLM** support)
category_summary_html:
A wave of major product launches dominated AI social media. OpenAI released GPT-5.4 mini and nano, new model tiers optimized for agents, coding, and computer use at 2x speed. Greg Brockman also announced subagent support in Codex, enabling parallelized autonomous workflows. Sam Altman reflected on the end of character-by-character coding, drawing 3.7M views.

  • Midjourney launched V8 in alpha testing — 5x faster with native 2K output, improved text rendering, and new creative modes — sparking extensive community discussion
  • Tri Dao released Mamba-3, the most powerful linear model to date, noting the frontier has shifted to hybrid architectures across Qwen, Kimi, and NVIDIA
  • Anthropic's Claude Skills guide went mega-viral (2.9M views), with detailed community engagement on reusable prompt patterns and workflow transformation
  • LangChain shipped a fully OSS coding agent, noting major engineering orgs like Stripe and Coinbase are building similar internal tools
  • Perplexity launched Comet Enterprise with MDM deployment and CrowdStrike integration, while Mistral announced Forge for enterprise model building and released Mistral Small 4 (119B MoE, day-0 vLLM support)

themes.description: Major Anthropic product launch: a comprehensive guide to Claude Skills (reusable prompt-based capabilities). The main post went mega-viral (2.9M views). trq212 (Anthropic employee) engaged extensively with community feedback, discussed skill patterns, subagents, hooks, marketplace concepts, and announced open-sourcing examples + livestream.

themes.description: GPT-5.4 Mini announcement, Codex subagents feature, Sam Altman teasing future products, and GPT-5.4 Pro capabilities discussion.

themes.description: Perplexity launches Comet Enterprise, an AI-native browser for enterprise teams with MDM deployment, CrowdStrike security integration, audit logs, and admin controls. Notable early adopters include AWS and Fortune.

themes.name: New Model Releases
themes.description: Mistral Small 4 (119B MoE, 256K context) released with day-0 vLLM support. OpenAI ships GPT-5.4 mini and nano optimized for agent pipelines. MiniCPM-o 4.5 gets vLLM support.

themes.description: Codex subagents, HF local coding agent CLI, debates about AI writing production code, and Altman's tribute to manual coders.

themes.description: RCT evidence showing GPT-4o tutor raising test scores by 0.15 SD. Context from researchers who also found negative effects of unstructured AI use.

items.title:
A much longer followup V8 post from our blog:

Hi everyone! Today we’re letting the community test a...
items.content:
A much longer followup V8 post from our blog:

Hi everyone! Today we’re letting the community test an early version of our V8 model on https://t.co/KwAgvvzJQk. We want your feedback and your help. Let’s explore what this new model can do.

V8 is an incredible model. It’s much better at following detailed directions and will still surprise you when you ask. Its ability to understand your aesthetics through personalization, style references (srefs) and moodboards is amazing. Images are more coherent and detailed. Text rendering works better than ever (when specified in “quotes”). And image generation is ~5x faster than before.

We’ve upgraded our web interfaces to support this speed. And more is coming. We have an improved conversation mode so you can just ‘talk’ in flow, a “Grid Mode” so you can focus on a single big set of images, and we’ve moved our settings into ‘sidebars’ so you can tweak settings without blocking your view.

V8 launches with support for multiple aspect ratios, --chaos, --weird, --exp, --raw. We support backwards compatibility with your V7 personalization profiles, moodboards, and srefs.

V8 brings a new --hd mode which natively renders images at 2K resolution and a --q 4 mode if you need a little bit of extra coherence.

Modes and Pricing: Presently, Relax is not yet supported. We are working on a new server cluster for Relax plus cheaper render modes. --HD, --Q4, SREF, and Moodboard jobs are 4x as slow as regular jobs and each costs 4x (for now).

The best way to help us improve V8 is to rate images in the lightbox. Click an image in create then click “I like this image” “I don't like this image” on as many of your V8 images as possible. You can use 1/2/3 hotkeys and arrow keys to rate images quickly.

Other Tips and Tricks: If you’re going for photos or a more ‘plain’ or ‘in control’ look we’d recommend switching to --raw right away or using moodboards or srefs to control your style. We’re still working on the ‘default’ aesthetics of V8, but we strongly encourage you to use your personalization and even crank it to –stylize 1000 asap. V8 is going to shine most right now when you rely heavily on our stylization systems and when you trend towards longer more specific prompting.

Please Note: This is an entirely new model with unique strengths and weaknesses, we want to learn from you what it's good and bad at but keep in mind it may require totally new prompting styles. So play around a bit!

It’s time to play! Show off what you’re making in #v8-showcase and let us know what you think in #ideas-and-feedback. As the model becomes more mature, we’ll do a community-wide roadmap ranking session to help us figure out what to prioritize next.

Thanks again for everyone’s help with the V8 pre-release rating parties, and thank you so much for being a part of Midjourney! Let’s have fun out there and find new wonders on this vast and shared sea of imagination.

items.title: Today, we’re introducing Forge, a system for enterprises to build frontier-grade AI models grounded ...
items.content:
Today, we’re introducing Forge, a system for enterprises to build frontier-grade AI models grounded in their proprietary knowledge.

🌎 Forge bridges the gap between generic AI and enterprise-specific needs. Instead of relying on broad, public data, organizations can train models that understand their internal context embedded within systems, workflows, and policies, aligning AI with their unique operations.

We have already partnered with world-leading organizations, like ASML, DSO National Laboratories Singapore, Ericsson, European Space Agency, Home Team Science and Technology Agency (HTX) Singapore and Reply to train models on the proprietary data that powers their most complex systems and future-defining technologies.

items.title:
OpenAI just shipped a full model tier for agents, and it's 2x faster.

GPT-5.4 mini and nano are two...
items.content:
OpenAI just shipped a full model tier for agents, and it's 2x faster.

GPT-5.4 mini and nano are two small, cost-efficient models built for coding, computer use, and running as subagents inside larger pipelines.

→ 2x faster than GPT-5 mini
→ Nano at $0.05/M input tokens
→ Mini handles multimodal tasks natively
→ Both live in ChatGPT, Codex, and the API

The key shift: these aren't just smaller versions. They're optimized to operate inside multi-agent systems, where one model orchestrates others to complete tasks end to end.

You can now build pipelines where fast, cheap subagents handle the heavy lifting without burning your budget on a flagship model.

https://t.co/pwfCkqmkuZ

items.summary: AlphaSignalAI reports OpenAI shipped GPT-5.4 mini and nano - small, cost-efficient models optimized for agents. 2x faster than GPT-5 mini, nano at $0.05/M input tokens, designed for multi-agent pipelines.

items.content_html:
OpenAI just shipped a full model tier for agents, and it's 2x faster.

GPT-5.4 mini and nano are two small, cost-efficient models built for coding, computer use, and running as subagents inside larger pipelines.

→ 2x faster than GPT-5 mini

→ Nano at $0.05/M input tokens

→ Mini handles multimodal tasks natively

→ Both live in ChatGPT, Codex, and the API

The key shift: these aren't just smaller versions. They're optimized to operate inside multi-agent systems, where one model orchestrates others to complete tasks end to end.

You can now build pipelines where fast, cheap subagents handle the heavy lifting without burning your budget on a flagship model.

https://t.co/pwfCkqmkuZ

Excited to share our latest work rethinking k-means for modern GPU architectures.

While the algorithm is classic, scaling it requires aggressively targeting memory bandwidth bottlenecks. Flash-KMeans introduces a strictly IO-aware design that achieves exact results with up to a 30x speedup over cuML and 200x over FAISS, completing million-scale iterations in milliseconds!

items.title:
This robotic hand can weave itself together in minutes.

Allonic developed a process that "braids" r...
items.content:
This robotic hand can weave itself together in minutes.

Allonic developed a process that "braids" robot bodies around a 3D-printed skeleton in a single automated step.

The tech draws from the textile industry, using braided fibers instead of traditional mechanical joints and bearings.

Their braiding system grows tendon-driven structures directly onto skeletal cores, creating flexible yet durable parts without screws or assembly.

One finger only needs 4 skeletal elements. Then thousands of braided fibers wrap around them to handle force and articulation.

The software converts designs directly into machine code that runs the braiding process.

But what matters is that robotic hands like this can cost up to $30,000, ~20-30% of a humanoid robot's total price.

Allonic's braiding process could crush those costs while enabling custom designs at scale.

items.summary: Rowan Cheung shares Allonic's braided robotic hand technology that weaves robot bodies around 3D-printed skeletons using textile industry techniques. Could dramatically reduce robotic hand costs from ~$30K.
items.reasoning: High engagement (52K views, 737 likes). Novel robotics manufacturing approach with significant cost implications for humanoid robots.

items.title:
One thing that makes me feel that code factory has not arrived yet is the following experiment:

1.A...
items.content:
One thing that makes me feel that code factory has not arrived yet is the following experiment:

1.Ask a LLM to do an in-depth rigorous review of your code
2. In a new thread, as same/different LLM  to consider those review comments independently and address issues it agrees with
3. Keep repeating until no new concerns

I find that this loop always goes on for a ridiculously long time, which means that there is a problem with the notion of claude-take-the-wheel.  This seems to happen no matter the harness or the specificity of the specs.  

It works fine for simple applications, but in the limit if the LLMs have this much cognitive dissonance you cannot trust it. 

Either this, or LLM are RLHFd to always find some kind of issue.
items.author: @HamelHusain
items.summary: Hamel Husain describes an experiment revealing AI coding limitations: asking LLMs to review code, then fix issues, then review again creates an endless loop of discovered issues - suggesting 'code factory' (fully autonomous coding) hasn't arrived. Notes this may be an RLHF artifact.
items.reasoning: Original and insightful observation about fundamental limitations of LLM-driven coding automation. Well-articulated experiment methodology revealing cognitive dissonance in LLMs. High engagement (200 likes, 20K views) from a respected ML practitioner. Important counter-narrative to autonomous coding hype.
items.summary_html: Hamel Husain describes an experiment revealing AI coding limitations: asking LLMs to review code, then fix issues, then review again creates an endless loop of discovered issues - suggesting 'code factory' (fully autonomous coding) hasn't arrived. Notes this may be an RLHF artifact.
items.content_html:
One thing that makes me feel that code factory has not arrived yet is the following experiment:

1.Ask a LLM to do an in-depth rigorous review of your code

2. In a new thread, as same/different LLM to consider those review comments independently and address issues it agrees with

3. Keep repeating until no new concerns

I find that this loop always goes on for a ridiculously long time, which means that there is a problem with the notion of claude-take-the-wheel. This seems to happen no matter the harness or the specificity of the specs.

It works fine for simple applications, but in the limit if the LLMs have this much cognitive dissonance you cannot trust it.

Either this, or LLM are RLHFd to always find some kind of issue.

items.title:
Visiting the NVIDIA GTC expo hall shows you why OpenAI is changing strategy.

All the big booths are...
items.content:
Visiting the NVIDIA GTC expo hall shows you why OpenAI is changing strategy.

All the big booths are enterprise.

Does this mean @sama is gonna give up his dreams of stealing Tim Cook’s lunch?

The biggest news here is how NVIDIA is bringing @openclaw to the enterprise.

The hidden message is “you don’t want to run your business on a Mac Mini, do you?”

Or, even more hidden: “a stack of DGX computers will be better for your development team since you can run an NVIDIA model on them and cut your token-generation bills with Anthropic down.”

But if I can discern these hidden messages at GTC so can others.

One guy sitting next to me remembers when NVIDIA was for gamers.

Barely a mention of video games here this year.

But the Holodeck team has a McLaren right in the middle of the NVIDIA booth. With a cool Apple Vision Pro app to do air flow analysis on a race car. Here is Max from NVIDIA telling me about it.

I have been telling everyone that Apple has the Formula 1 exclusive, which will keep Apple relevant even if OpenAI went consumer.

Enterprise is the smart move. That video game stuff was last week at GDC.

I am doing a ton of video this week for Chinese media and investors: https://t.co/2BjIVhqvR4

OpenClaw is even hotter in China than in USA. 

Why?

An analyst I met explained it this way: the Chinese heard that Anthropic hates them, so are choosing systems that let them use their own models.

Global politics are everywhere on the show floor.

Oh, and same guy said he knows developers who are spending $1,000 a day with their OpenClaws. That is certainly enterprise.

Consumers look at you like you have a mental illness if you try to tell them they need to spend $1,000 a day on tokens.

But someone is, which is why Jensen said he  raised his revenue projections to a trillion dollars.

Ask your enterprise developers are throwing cash into the AI factories to get those tokens out. 

What an industry.

Enterprise is sexy all of a sudden. Didn’t see that coming.

items.title: BREAKING: Reliability, which I have been harping on here since 2019, continues to be deep problem, e...
items.content:
BREAKING: Reliability, which I have been harping on here since 2019, continues to be deep problem, even with the latest models.

A new @Princeton review below offers a taxonomy of some of the many ways in which reliability continues to haunt LLMs seven years and a trillion dollars later.

Crucially, “many models lack metacognition about their own reliability”. They don’t know what they don’t know.  

Forget about AGI if you can’t solve that problem.

It’s past time to rethink the whole LLM paradigm.

items.title:
how does our OSS coding agent compare to others that Ramp, Stripe, Coinbase built?

In order to comp...
items.content:
how does our OSS coding agent compare to others that Ramp, Stripe, Coinbase built?

In order to compare, we'll use this excellent analysis from @kishan_dahya (https://t.co/R8yzlHQlEM) to break down the components:

Harness: we use deepagents under the hood

Sandboxes: bring your own! we use our new LangSmith sandboxes, but also integrate with @e2b @daytonaio 

Tools: a few basic ones (websearch, etc) on top of standard deepagents ones

Context engineering: agents.md

Orchestration: subagents and middleware

Invocation: slack, linear, github

Validation: all inside the agent

🗺️ Hoodmaps for 🏡 Airbn...
items.content:
This was asked for for YEARS and I could never find time to build it myself

🗺️ Hoodmaps for 🏡 Airbnb

Hoodmaps is my app that lets you find out where to stay in a city, it classifies neighborhoods by: 
🟥 Tourists
🟨 Cool
🟩 Rich
🟦 Suits
⬜️ Normies

I asked Claude Code to build it and it kinda works, not perfect but a start

I just need to get the map to update faster and then publish it as a Chrome extension

For now you can try it though:

https://t.co/hVQKlD6QEV

Copy paste that in console on Airbnb map, type your city as a slug (like los-angeles) and it should work

Happy booking!!!

items.title: @chrislakin This one took me longer than most, I wrote it over 1.5 weeks (though obviously doing a l...
items.content:
@chrislakin This one took me longer than most, I wrote it over 1.5 weeks (though obviously doing a lot more alongside).

Claude does a lot of research for me, e.g. it researched and categorized all our skills and helps generate a bunch of examples. I had it do a first pass which helps me structure my thought, but I think I threw out basically all of the copy for it.

It does generate all of the images, though I usually have it do multiple takes and I'll choose one.

As many have said, writing is an exploratory process that forces you to think and distill ideas. I try and only publish things where I feel like there are genuine insights, which can be tough. 

My friend @aadilpickle says when writing "You have nothing until you have everything". I think you can tell when you have something and I felt like I didn't have anything until like like a week into the process.

Emphasizes writing as exploratory thinking that forces idea distillation. Quotes friend: 'You have nothing until you have everything.'
items.content_html:
@chrislakin This one took me longer than most, I wrote it over 1.5 weeks (though obviously doing a lot more alongside).

Claude does a lot of research for me, e.g. it researched and categorized all our skills and helps generate a bunch of examples. I had it do a first pass which helps me structure my thought, but I think I threw out basically all of the copy for it.

It does generate all of the images, though I usually have it do multiple takes and I'll choose one.

As many have said, writing is an exploratory process that forces you to think and distill ideas. I try and only publish things where I feel like there are genuine insights, which can be tough.

My friend @aadilpickle says when writing "You have nothing until you have everything". I think you can tell when you have something and I felt like I didn't have anything until like like a week into the process.

items.title:
Google just gave every free user a personal AI that knows your whole life.

Personal Intelligence is...
items.content:
Google just gave every free user a personal AI that knows your whole life.

Personal Intelligence is now free in Gemini and Chrome, connecting Gmail, Photos, YouTube, and Search to answer questions only you could ask.

→ Finds your car's tire size from a photo
→ Plans trips using your actual past travel
→ Recommends based on your real purchase history

The trick: your data never gets stuffed into the model. It selectively pulls only the most relevant context when needed, solving the "too much data, too little context" problem.

You can now ask questions without explaining yourself every time.
https://t.co/jmE7c6Ws8g

items.title:
Add this to your Codex custom instructions for a way better experience:

"When communicating your re...
items.content:
Add this to your Codex custom instructions for a way better experience:

"When communicating your results back to me, explain what you did and what happened in plain, clear English. Avoid jargon, technical implementation details, and code-speak in your final responses. Write as if you're explaining to a smart person who isn't looking at the code. Your actual work (how you think, plan, write code, debug, and solve problems) should stay fully technical and rigorous. This only applies to how you talk to me about it.

Before reporting back to me, if at all possible, verify your own work. Don't just write code and assume it's done. Actually test it using the tools available to you. If possible, run it, check the output, and confirm it does what was asked. If you're building something visual like a web app, view the pages, click through the flows, and check that things render and behave correctly. If you're writing a script, run it against real or representative input and inspect the results. If there are edge cases you can simulate, try them.

Define finishing criteria for yourself before you start: what does "done" look like for this task? Use that as your checklist before you come back to me. If something fails or looks off, fix it and re-test. Don't just flag it and hand it back. The goal is to keep me out of the loop on iteration. I want to receive finished, working results, not a first draft that needs me to spot-check it. Only come back to me when you've confirmed things work, or when you've genuinely hit a wall that requires my input."
items.author: @mattshumer_
items.summary: Matt Shumer shares detailed custom instructions for OpenAI Codex that emphasize: plain English communication, self-verification of work, defining completion criteria, and keeping humans out of iteration loops
items.reasoning: Highly practical and well-crafted prompt engineering for Codex. 452 likes and 33K views indicate strong resonance. Useful template for anyone using coding agents

This only applies to how you talk to me about it.

Before reporting back to me, if at all possible, verify your own work. Don't just write code and assume it's done. Actually test it using the tools available to you. If possible, run it, check the output, and confirm it does what was asked. If you're building something visual like a web app, view the pages, click through the flows, and check that things render and behave correctly. If you're writing a script, run it against real or representative input and inspect the results. If there are edge cases you can simulate, try them.

Define finishing criteria for yourself before you start: what does "done" look like for this task? Use that as your checklist before you come back to me. If something fails or looks off, fix it and re-test. Don't just flag it and hand it back. The goal is to keep me out of the loop on iteration. 9/10

Just like similar optimizations to trade off training for inference have been made for Transformers - most notably DeepSeek's Latent Attention - Mamba-3 opens up a new design space of tradeoffs.
8/10

items.title: With Mamba-3, we focused on its inference capabilities, motivated by the rise of agents and inferenc...
items.content:
With Mamba-3, we focused on its inference capabilities, motivated by the rise of agents and inference-heavy post training. Performance and efficiency are always tradeoffs, and with Mamba-3 we focused on the capabilities-to-inference-efficiency Pareto frontier.

By sweeping the model's state size - a coarse approx of a recurrent model's inference speed, Mamba-3 Pareto dominates previous linear models.
3/10

Mistral AI ju...
items.content:
Stop renting generic AI. You can now train a frontier model on your own private data.

Mistral AI just launched Forge, a platform to build custom AI models from your internal systems and workflows.

→ Runs fully on-prem, your data never leaves
→ Trained on your code, docs, policies
→ Model size cut 2-3x via distillation
→ Already live at ESA, Ericsson, ASML

Instead of prompting a generic model, Forge trains from scratch on proprietary data. Think fine-tuning, but deeper: weights actually encode your internal knowledge.

Software teams can now ship a model that understands their own codebase, not just public GitHub.
https://t.co/DTrLkhNlIK

Instead of prompting a generic model, Forge trains from scratch on proprietary data. Think fine-tuning, but deeper: weights actually encode your internal knowledge.

Software teams can now ship a model that understands their own codebase, not just public GitHub.

https://t.co/DTrLkhNlIK

items.title:
Google just turned 5 million news articles into a flood prediction system. 

The research team built...
items.content:
Google just turned 5 million news articles into a flood prediction system. 

The research team built Groundsource, a pipeline using Gemini to extract flood data from global news.

> 2.6M flood events mapped
> 24-hour urban flash flood forecasts
> Coverage across 80 languages 

It runs news articles through Gemini and pulls out structured flood records, then maps them to real coordinates. 

1. Check if the article describes a real flood
2. Extract the exact dates the flood happened
3. Pull specific locations like streets or neighborhoods
4. Match those locations to standard geographic codes 

The result is a structured, geo-tagged dataset that hydrological models can actually train on.

This opens up flood risk coverage for cities that had no historical data before. 

Researchers can now build and improve prediction models for regions previously invisible to early-warning systems.

items.title:
Let's be honest, most people ain't checking the code they're writing with AI.

Yeah, it may be impor...
items.content:
Let's be honest, most people ain't checking the code they're writing with AI.

Yeah, it may be important, and we are always yapping about how you gotta do it, but there's absolutely zero chance most people are taking the time to slow down and go line by line, trying to understand everything the model wrote.

It's just too easy to produce a billion lines of code now.

It's even easier to test the product (rather than the code) and ask the model to fix whatever you don't like.

And you know what? I think not looking at the code is fine for many.

I've seen a lot of code 100% written by humans. Some of it is an immense pile of garbage, and nobody has died.

100% AI-generated is actually an improvement for those products.

But there are many places that can't afford slop, and we always take things too far.

Non-supervised AI-generated code is dangerous.

There will be some blowback, and companies will start getting very wary of AI cowboys. Some might outright ban AI-generated code, and some will figure out how to make developers liable for their code.

Your AI agent can now run code in the cloud, without touching your machine.

Google just open-source...
items.content:
Your AI agent can now run code in the cloud, without touching your machine.

Google just open-sourced the Colab MCP Server, letting any AI agent write and execute code directly inside a Colab notebook.

→ Works with Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, Gemini
→ Full notebook lifecycle control: build, run, visualize
→ Sandboxed cloud execution, nothing local at risk

MCP acts like a universal plug. Your agent speaks to it, it controls Colab. No custom glue code, no manual copy-pasting between your terminal and a notebook cell.

Training a model but your laptop can't handle it? Point your agent at free cloud GPUs and let it run.

https://t.co/1mYi7990re

Stop paying for cloud GPUs. You can now train LLMs locally with a full web UI.

Unsloth AI just launched Unsloth Studio, an open-source tool to train and run models on your own machine.

→ 2x faster training, 70% less VRAM
→ 500+ supported models
→ Auto-builds datasets from PDFs, CSVs
→ Self-healing tool calling built-in

It works by combining optimized kernels with smart memory reuse, cutting overhead without touching model quality.

Fine-tuning a custom model went from a cloud job to a single pip install.

items.title: The primary issue is that despite being known for compute efficiency in terms of raw FLOPs-to-perfor...
items.content:
The primary issue is that despite being known for compute efficiency in terms of raw FLOPs-to-performance, linear models are not very hardware efficient during *decoding* due to the fixed-size state.
Mamba-2 only hits ~2.5 arithmetic intensity, whereas matmul for H100s peaks at around 300.
This leaves lots of compute idle!
5/10
items.author: @tri_dao
items.summary: Tri Dao explains the hardware efficiency problem of linear models during decoding: Mamba-2 hits only ~2.5 arithmetic intensity vs H100's peak of ~300.
items.reasoning: Important framing of the problem Mamba-3 solves. Quantitative insight into hardware utilization gap.

items.title: If we *really* care about inference speed, and are willing to spend more train time to maximize the ...
items.content:
If we *really* care about inference speed, and are willing to spend more train time to maximize the model's perf at a fixed decoding threshold, what else can we do?
Mamba-3 introduces a variant that deviates from the single-input single-output (SISO) structure of predecessors - what Mamba-1 called state expansion.
4/10

Mamba-3 introduces a variant that deviates from the single-input single-output (SISO) structure of predecessors - what Mamba-1 called state expansion.

4/10

items.title: One of the hardest problems with using AI agents to automate meaningful document work (contracts, KY...
items.content:
One of the hardest problems with using AI agents to automate meaningful document work (contracts, KYC, diligence, claims, and more) is not actually building the agent, but building the UI/UX audit trail so the human can understand decisions linking back to the source documents - in PDFs, Powerpoint, Word, etc.

This requires really good layout detection and segmentation, that you need to surface as *metadata context* to the AI agent, beyond any document -> markdown conversion.

We've built really-nice VLM-capabilities to identify and segment all the elements within your document: from tables to charts to form boxes. You can use this metadata to directly provide experiences where humans can analyze the source document even as decisions are being made.

Come check out LlamaParse! https://t.co/TqP6OT5U5O
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: Jerry Liu (LlamaIndex) discusses the hardest problem in AI document automation: building UI/UX audit trails linking AI decisions back to source documents, promoting LlamaParse's VLM-based layout detection capabilities
items.reasoning: Interesting insight about the real bottleneck in document AI being UX rather than agent building, but ultimately promotional for LlamaParse
items.summary_html: Jerry Liu (LlamaIndex) discusses the hardest problem in AI document automation: building UI/UX audit trails linking AI decisions back to source documents, promoting LlamaParse's VLM-based layout detection capabilities
items.content_html:
One of the hardest problems with using AI agents to automate meaningful document work (contracts, KYC, diligence, claims, and more) is not actually building the agent, but building the UI/UX audit trail so the human can understand decisions linking back to the source documents - in PDFs, Powerpoint, Word, etc.

This requires really good layout detection and segmentation, that you need to surface as *metadata context* to the AI agent, beyond any document -> markdown conversion.

We've built really-nice VLM-capabilities to identify and segment all the elements within your document: from tables to charts to form boxes. You can use this metadata to directly provide experiences where humans can analyze the source document even as decisions are being made.

Come check out LlamaParse! https://t.co/TqP6OT5U5O

items.title:
Stop tab-switching between Docs, Sheets, and Drive. An AI agent just replaced all of it.

Manus inte...
items.content:
Stop tab-switching between Docs, Sheets, and Drive. An AI agent just replaced all of it.

Manus integrated Google Workspace CLI, letting it edit your entire Workspace from one prompt.

→ Replies to Doc comments automatically
→ Updates specific spreadsheet cells
→ Reorganizes Drive folders
→ Renames Slides titles

The CLI reads Google's Discovery Service at runtime, so it dynamically builds every available command. No hardcoded API wrappers, no custom glue code.

Building agents that touch real productivity data no longer means writing OAuth flows by hand.
https://t.co/zA3Txe63LW

items.title: Not maintainable by whom? I have two large web apps (more than 1M tokens in the codebase), both perf...

Not maintainable by whom? I have two large web apps (more than 1M tokens in the codebase), both perfectly maintainable by agentic coders.

Without access to agentic coders, I:

1. Wouldn't get all the features my web apps have.

2. Couldn't maintain two big web apps as a single person.

items.title:
We hosted an executive dinner at NVIDIA GTC with @Modular.

600+ people signed up, and we had to tur...
items.content:
We hosted an executive dinner at NVIDIA GTC with @Modular.

600+ people signed up, and we had to turn away more than 500. We had a packed house 🔥

This is one of the only dinners where we discuss the impact of AI agents across the entire stack - from infrastructure/systems to context and agentic engineering. Some of the topics we discussed:
- Is the future composed of general models or specialized models?
- Are agents a systems problem or model problem?
- How are non-technical users adopting agent tooling like Claude Cowork?
- Where do humans spend the most time on repetitive document work?

Massive thank you to the Modular team for partnering with @llama_index on this, and to every single person who showed up.

To the 500+ on the waitlist - next time we're getting a bigger venue.
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: Jerry Liu recaps an executive dinner at NVIDIA GTC co-hosted with Modular, discussing AI agents across the stack - 600+ signups, topics included general vs specialized models, agents as systems vs model problems, and non-technical adoption of Claude Cowork

We hosted an executive dinner at NVIDIA GTC with @Modular.

600+ people signed up, and we had to turn away more than 500. We had a packed house 🔥

This is one of the only dinners where we discuss the impact of AI agents across the entire stack - from infrastructure/systems to context and agentic engineering. Some of the topics we discussed:

  • Is the future composed of general models or specialized models?
  • Are agents a systems problem or model problem?
  • How are non-technical users adopting agent tooling like Claude Cowork?
  • Where do humans spend the most time on repetitive document work?

Massive thank you to the Modular team for partnering with @llama_index on this, and to every single person who showed up.

To the 500+ on the waitlist - next time we're getting a bigger venue.

items.title: The removed comment implied that my web apps have no users and this is why I wrongly think AI can wr...
items.content:
The removed comment implied that my web apps have no users and this is why I wrongly think AI can write production code.

Those folks, in their coping, went from "it will never write an entire app" to "it will never write a maintainable app" to "it will never write a scalable app."

I guess their next line of defense will be that "it will never write an app anyone would pay for," and then "it will never write an app that would solve world hunger" or "it will never write an app that will control a rocket or a nuclear station," as if those folks write such software on a daily basis.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov defends AI's ability to write production code, noting critics keep moving goalposts from 'can't write an app' to 'can't write a maintainable app' to 'can't write a scalable app.'
items.reasoning: Interesting observation about shifting goalposts in AI coding skepticism. Moderate engagement. Relevant to the AI coding capabilities debate.
items.summary_html: Burkov defends AI's ability to write production code, noting critics keep moving goalposts from 'can't write an app' to 'can't write a maintainable app' to 'can't write a scalable app.'
items.content_html:
The removed comment implied that my web apps have no users and this is why I wrongly think AI can write production code.

Those folks, in their coping, went from "it will never write an entire app" to "it will never write a maintainable app" to "it will never write a scalable app."

I guess their next line of defense will be that "it will never write an app anyone would pay for," and then "it will never write an app that would solve world hunger" or "it will never write an app that will control a rocket or a nuclear station," as if those folks write such software on a daily basis.

items.content:
Your browser just became your entire work stack.

Perplexity launched Comet Enterprise, an AI-native browser built for teams at scale.

→ Deploy to thousands of devices via MDM
→ Full audit logs and telemetry for admins
→ CrowdStrike Falcon blocks phishing and malware live
→ Fortune, AWS, Bessemer already running it

The browser sits on secure infrastructure with granular admin controls. IT teams manage access, usage, and visibility without new tooling.

Security isn't bolted on. It's baked into every session, at the browser layer.
https://t.co/fgXYB8FXiX

items.title:
Stop writing docs for your AI agents. They can now learn any tool from scratch.

HyperSkill is an op...
items.content:
Stop writing docs for your AI agents. They can now learn any tool from scratch.

HyperSkill is an open-source tool that reads live web docs and outputs a structured SKILL.md your agent can navigate.

→ Any topic or URL as input
→ Drop a single file into your project
→ Works with coding agents and automation workflows

It searches the web, scrapes the docs, then uses GPT-4o to structure everything into a clean skill tree.

Any agent that reads a file can now onboard itself to any library or API, instantly.
https://t.co/qMNrCxAC0d

items.title:
@sd_marlow xAI and SpaceX are merging. 

SpaceX is putting datacenters into space and has the distri...
items.content:
@sd_marlow xAI and SpaceX are merging. 

SpaceX is putting datacenters into space and has the distribution method, with Starlink, to move packets around the earth. xAI is providing LLMs and other AIs to run robots and Robotaxis and social networks. The two together make up a sizeable force in AI. 

The odd duck out at the moment is Tesla, which makes the software that drives cars, and manipulates the robots after xAI's Grok gives both commands. 

I won't be shocked if all three join together at some point.

items.summary: Scobleizer discusses potential xAI-SpaceX merger and speculates about Tesla joining, with SpaceX putting datacenters in space and xAI providing LLMs for robots and robotaxis

@sd_marlow xAI and SpaceX are merging.

SpaceX is putting datacenters into space and has the distribution method, with Starlink, to move packets around the earth. xAI is providing LLMs and other AIs to run robots and Robotaxis and social networks. The two together make up a sizeable force in AI.

The odd duck out at the moment is Tesla, which makes the software that drives cars, and manipulates the robots after xAI's Grok gives both commands.

I won't be shocked if all three join together at some point.

items.title:
I thought I knew everything about autonomous vehicles, but, nope.

I almost got in an argument with ...
items.content:
I thought I knew everything about autonomous vehicles, but, nope.

I almost got in an argument with cofounder @dfergusonnz  of @nuro about LiDAR at NVIDIA GTC during his session. He laid out that LiDAR shows safety improvements. But then said that LiDAR can see a kid all in black at night lying on the road around a turn. My answer? Matrix headlights see all that. 

Truth be told, his session at GTC was awesome and Nuro has amazing tech that can generalize to any vehicle. He showed it off driving in Japan for the first time driving on a left hand road it had never seen before. 

But seriously, been doing a ton of work today to understand the autonomous vehicle industry. Talked with NVIDIA's team, met people from General Motors, Uber, and others, attended a class by Nuro's founder, and on Friday NVIDIA is taking me for a ride.

The thing that stunned me was NVIDIA's simulator. You give it a picture of a road and a prompt and it creates a simulated road for you. Then you can make it snow, rain, or any number of situations. Put pedestrians on. 

And all created with a fairly small model. 

Now I understand why NVIDIA is in love with simulators.

I asked Jensen at his press conference about it. And what it means for glasses coming soon. And he was bullish on the future of glasses to show both ground truth (reality) and whatever you want the simulator to show you (the Holodeck). 

GTC 2030 is gonna be insane. :-)

Videos will come someday. I have a ton of them and no time to edit or upload. Packed the week with too many meetings. 

Be back over the weekend.

Or I might steal one of those simulators. The whole thing was running on a box under the demo station. You think @AlexFinn has cool inferencing computers under his desk? A Mac can't simulate any road, with any condition, and his Mac doesn't have a steering wheel. 

New goals. :-)

Oh, and the conclusion (yes I'm burying the lede, as we say in journalism circles): Pretty much car company will have self driving cars within 18 months. 

Which is awesome. Fewer lives lost.

The real question is who can do Robotaxi. Nuro is only planning on a few tens of thousands of vehicles. That's not nearly aggressive enough for me. Guy who works for GM was sitting next to me and he said "the companies can't figure out the liability and regulation issues yet to really scale up."

Back to the argument, he said LiDARs cost in "low thousands" now. In the car industry that's a huge amount, as one analyst that I didn't catch name of, pointed out in the audience. 

Everyone I'm talking to says Tesla drives smoother than the others. Will see on Friday when I get my ride in NVIDIA's.

My money is still on Tesla. They have all the pieces to do Robotaxi at scale and become the "Starbucks" of Robotaxis. 

But this market is just starting, so the next 18 months are gonna be highly entertaining. 

More arguments ahead.

So honored to be reporting for CNQQ. Chinese Tech ETF. Investors in China are highly interested in autonomous vehicles. https://t.co/2BjIVhqvR4

Videos will come someday. I have a ton of them and no time to edit or upload. Packed the week with too many meetings.

Be back over the weekend.

Or I might steal one of those simulators. The whole thing was running on a box under the demo station. You think @AlexFinn has cool inferencing computers under his desk? A Mac can't simulate any road, with any condition, and his Mac doesn't have a steering wheel.

New goals. :-)

Oh, and the conclusion (yes I'm burying the lede, as we say in journalism circles): Pretty much car company will have self driving cars within 18 months.

Which is awesome. Fewer lives lost.

The real question is who can do Robotaxi. Nuro is only planning on a few tens of thousands of vehicles. That's not nearly aggressive enough for me. Guy who works for GM was sitting next to me and he said "the companies can't figure out the liability and regulation issues yet to really scale up."

Back to the argument, he said LiDARs cost in "low thousands" now. In the car industry that's a huge amount, as one analyst that I didn't catch name of, pointed out in the audience.

Everyone I'm talking to says Tesla drives smoother than the others. Will see on Friday when I get my ride in NVIDIA's.

My money is still on Tesla. They have all the pieces to do Robotaxi at scale and become the "Starbucks" of Robotaxis.

But this market is just starting, so the next 18 months are gonna be highly entertaining.

More arguments ahead.

So honored to be reporting for CNQQ. Chinese Tech ETF. Investors in China are highly interested in autonomous vehicles. https://t.co/2BjIVhqvR4

items.title:
Our bottleneck for some of the biggest change ahead may be humans. 

I shared the story of Paul Cony...
items.content:
Our bottleneck for some of the biggest change ahead may be humans. 

I shared the story of Paul Conyngham and his dog Rosie on my Instagram when it broke. 

When I read the coverage, my shock was less that ChatGPT finding a possible treatment (though to be sure, I loved it) and more that UNSW scientists got on board and were somehow able to get the vaccine made and used in 2 months. 

The companies who gain the most value with AI will also have the leaders who figure out how to speed up human and physical processes. 

Think: trials, approvals, testing, drug manufacturing, and dog belly rubs. 

Though I believe I’m fastest in market on that last one.

items.title: The corporate advisory boards that I have been a part of have almost exclusively been “vibe checks”,...
items.content:
The corporate advisory boards that I have been a part of have almost exclusively been “vibe checks”, where presentations are made about work the company is doing, and the advisory panel chats about things for a while.

This seems like a poor way to utilize the advisors.

Maybe it is worth it just for a rare outburst of “What? No! Are you insane?” But if the company executives don’t actually do something differently than what they were already planning, then the advisory board was actually wasted.

The right time to use advisors is when there is an open and contentious question, and you want an opinion from an unbiased expert. You probably don’t want to convene a “board of advisors”, but rather draw from a “pool of advisors” according to their skillsets.

items.title: Hayek’s classic insight was that much economically relevant knowledge is dispersed, local, and often...
items.content:
Hayek’s classic insight was that much economically relevant knowledge is dispersed, local, and often tacit. 

That is one reason decentralized markets have historically outperformed central planning.

But AI can change those “knowledge physics” by making more of that knowledge codifiable, transferable, and usable at scale.

items.content:
I just recorded a video at NVIDIA GTC with Jensen Huang about this.

OpenClaw allows AI agents to:

• plan complex tasks
• access enterprise tools
• run code
• call models
• interact with systems
• spawn sub-agents

AI is no longer just answering questions.

It can execute work.

items.title:
Everyone is obsessing over AI models and cloud GPUs.

But the next AI winners might be decided by so...
items.content:
Everyone is obsessing over AI models and cloud GPUs.

But the next AI winners might be decided by something far less hyped:

Mobile networks.

At Mobile World Congress, I sat down with @OneLinders, Head of Thought Leadership at @ericsson to unpack why connectivity, not just compute, will determine who actually monetizes AI.

Here’s what most leaders are missing…

#MWC26 #EricssonMWC Ericsson Ambassador @EricssonNA
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon's thread opener about how mobile networks (not just models/GPUs) may determine AI monetization winners, based on MWC26 discussion with Ericsson
items.reasoning: Original thread from MWC26 with contrarian thesis that connectivity infrastructure is underappreciated in AI value chain

items.title: Industrial AI is entering a new phase where experimentation gives space to real operational deployme...
items.content:
Industrial AI is entering a new phase where experimentation gives space to real operational deployment.

items.title:
Inference is also shifting.

Today it’s centralized.
Tomorrow it gets distributed.

Closer to the ed...
items.content:
Inference is also shifting.

Today it’s centralized.
Tomorrow it gets distributed.

Closer to the edge.
Closer to the device.
Closer to the user.

We saw this movie with video.

It started centralized. Then usage exploded, and distribution moved outward.

AI will follow a similar path.

The operators who rethink uplink, latency, and pricing models now will have the monetization advantage.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon argues AI inference will shift from centralized to distributed/edge, similar to video distribution evolution
items.reasoning: Interesting analogy comparing AI inference distribution to video CDN evolution; practical insight about uplink, latency, and pricing implications

items.title:
Engineering Agentic AI 

Over the past few months, there's been a significant focus on AI agents and...
items.content:
Engineering Agentic AI 

Over the past few months, there's been a significant focus on AI agents and their development. Back in September, people predicted that agents would become a major topic, and honestly, Gartner cycle hasn't even reached the peak of agentic hype yet. So, it's reasonable to expect that the term will be stretched, exaggerated, and potentially overused by spring. Nonetheless, embracing the concept of agentic development is both powerful and practical for driving business results through AI technology.

As we look ahead, the demand for flexibility in AI application development is becoming increasingly clear. While platforms like Salesforce's Agentforce and tools like Amazon Q Developer and Google Agentspace provide robust agentic capabilities, they may not cater to every specific need. This is where AI development frameworks come into play, offering a more adaptable approach to building various types of AI applications. These frameworks are designed to empower developers by providing the necessary tools and resources to create custom solutions that can be tailored to specific use cases, ultimately enhancing innovation in the AI space.

The rise of AI development frameworks (ADFs) is increasingly significant for enterprise AI. These frameworks facilitate collaboration among various stakeholders, not just developers, which fosters the creation of innovative AI solutions. By streamlining the development process, ADFs allow developers to concentrate on building applications rather than getting caught up in repetitive tasks.

Moreover, ADFs establish consistent standards that enhance governance and maintainability across AI applications. They also support scalability by providing reusable components, making it simpler for organizations to expand their AI capabilities. In addition, ADFs play a vital role in ensuring compliance with regulatory and ethical standards, promoting responsible usage of AI technologies. The flexibility allows businesses to stay current with advancements in AI without needing extensive system overhauls. Overall, ADFs mark a transformative move towards more efficient and standardized AI application development, driving innovation within enterprises.

AI Development Frameworks Components

Practioner services - This is a product category of "development" framework instead of a "developer" framework. This distinction emphasizes the diverse roles interacting with an ADF (Application Development Framework). It highlights Google’s Vertex and its support for various tools, such as an IDE, Python notebooks, and a no-code toolset called Agentspace. Additionally, the framework encompasses a range of functionalities including testing spaces, workflow designers, orchestration tools, and advanced prompt engineering tools, making it versatile in its utility.

Data and Integration Services - These not only drive how data enters a model through techniques like RAG but also facilitate data sharing between models within an agentic application. Furthermore, with the increasing presence of machine-based integrations with agents—similar to Nvidia’s CES keynote—this layer will encompass interactions with edge and hybrid deployments.

Model Services - In many ways, the heart of the ADF. They enable ADFs to integrate applications with multiple models, providing comprehensive support for evaluating and comparing these models. This ensures that developers can select the right model based on the application’s needs. Additionally, this area has recently seen significant innovation, including enhanced fine-tuning, multimodel support, and model distillation.

Management Security and Governance — As is often the case, this component of the model remains somewhat abstract. It is essential that we achieve a high degree of integration between ADFs and the IT control plane. I firmly believe that although an ADF should incorporate role-based access control to some extent, the management of these authorizations should be delegated to more robust solutions. As we approach early 2025, it is clear that this aspect of ADFs presents a significant opportunity for enhancement and optimization.

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #IoT #IIoT #PyTorch #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #Java #JavaScript #ReactJS #GoLang #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode

References

Anderson, J. (2025, February 11). Are AI development frameworks the foundation of the agentic world? Forbes. Retrieved February 11, 2025, from https://t.co/AVjRCtIoGt

Yang, Z., Zhou, Z., Wang, S., Cong, X., Han, X., Yan, Y., Liu, Z., Tan, Z., Liu, P., Yu, D., Liu, Z., Shi, X., & Sun, M. (2024, March 19). MatPlotAgent: Method and evaluation for LLM-based agentic scientific data visualization. arXiv. Retrieved February 11, 2025, from https://t.co/iStioFf4NH

items.title:
Funded start-ups building the same product become competitors.

Indie start-ups building the same pr...
items.content:
Funded start-ups building the same product become competitors.

Indie start-ups building the same product become friends.

Indie start-ups only need $10k MRR to succeed. The market is so big that it is unrealistic and unnecessary for two independent developers to “compete” with each other.

Both can support each other and win together.

The only rule applies: have your own touch and vision for your product. Don't be that annoying copycat. Build wealth on your own effort and talent.

My biggest takeaway:

Cloud may be the engine.
AI may be the brain.
But mobile networks are becoming the nervous system.

And nervous systems decide how fast, secure, and intelligent the body can act.

If you are building AI products or investing in infrastructure, this shift matters.

When AI agents act on real-time data, three things become mission critical:

→ Reliable connectivity
→ Secure data flows
→ Low latency

If your network drops, lags, or gets compromised, the AI fails.

This is why “AI-ready” is not just about GPUs.

It is about differentiated connectivity, powered by:

→ Network APIs
→ Network slicing
→ Edge execution

When AI agents act on real-time data, three things become mission critical:

→ Reliable connectivity

→ Secure data flows

If your network drops, lags, or gets compromised, the AI fails.

This is why “AI-ready” is not just about GPUs.

It is about differentiated connectivity, powered by:

→ Network APIs

→ Network slicing

→ Edge execution

items.title:
What if a single text prompt could generate an entire animated 3D scene?

Not a concept.

A real sce...
items.content:
What if a single text prompt could generate an entire animated 3D scene?

Not a concept.

A real scene you can edit and export into production tools.

AI is starting to compress what used to be an entire VFX pipeline into one step.

I recently explored how this works with @autodesk.

And the implications for creative production are huge.

Let’s break it down.

items.title:
Right now AI = centralized cloud.

Text in, text out.
Massive models.
Huge data centers.

But that’s...
items.content:
Right now AI = centralized cloud.

Text in, text out.
Massive models.
Huge data centers.

But that’s the first chapter.

We’re moving toward:

→ Multimodal AI
→ AI agents
→ Physical AI embedded in devices

That changes everything.

Because now AI depends on real-time data, not static datasets.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon describes shift from centralized cloud AI to multimodal AI, AI agents, and physical AI embedded in devices requiring real-time data

items.content_html:
Right now AI = centralized cloud.

Text in, text out.

Massive models.

Huge data centers.

But that’s the first chapter.

We’re moving toward:

→ Multimodal AI

→ AI agents

→ Physical AI embedded in devices

That changes everything.

Because now AI depends on real-time data, not static datasets.

items.reasoning: Thoughtful metaphor about AI infrastructure and orchestration, but low engagement and conversational context
items.summary_html: Ant Grasso replies to Salesforce/BCG about AI infrastructure using railway analogy - intelligent services running on infrastructure, value depends on orchestration within business processes
items.content_html:
@salesforce @BCG Thank you for the interesting perspective.

The railway analogy aligns well with this view, because what we will increasingly see is a growing number of intelligent services running on top of that infrastructure, enabling companies to operate their own trains with greater autonomy and consistency.

Over time, real value will depend on how effectively these services are orchestrated within business processes.

Another important piece is interoperability.

Once the AI generates the scene, it can export via USD...
items.content:
Another important piece is interoperability.

Once the AI generates the scene, it can export via USD into tools like:

→ Maya
→ Blender
→ Unreal Engine
→ 3ds Max

AI becomes the scene creation layer, while traditional tools remain the refinement layer.

items.title:
What surprised me most is how much of the technical pipeline can now be automated.

AI systems can h...
items.content:
What surprised me most is how much of the technical pipeline can now be automated.

AI systems can handle things like:

→ motion capture generation
→ camera tracking
→ alpha masks
→ character passes

The result is simple.

Less time preparing scenes.
More time iterating ideas.

items.content_html:
What surprised me most is how much of the technical pipeline can now be automated.

AI systems can handle things like:

→ motion capture generation

→ camera tracking

→ alpha masks

→ character passes

The result is simple.

Less time preparing scenes.

More time iterating ideas.

items.title:
The traditional workflow for a 3D scene looks something like this:

→ asset modeling
→ motion captur...
items.content:
The traditional workflow for a 3D scene looks something like this:

→ asset modeling
→ motion capture
→ camera tracking
→ animation passes
→ compositing layers

Each step adds time and complexity.

Now AI can generate editable 3D assets directly from:

→ text prompts
→ uploaded images

Which means creators start with the idea, not the pipeline.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon contrasts traditional 3D workflow steps with AI's ability to generate editable 3D assets from text prompts or images
items.reasoning: Describes the paradigm shift in 3D content creation
items.summary_html: Ronald van Loon contrasts traditional 3D workflow steps with AI's ability to generate editable 3D assets from text prompts or images
items.content_html:
The traditional workflow for a 3D scene looks something like this:

→ asset modeling

→ motion capture

→ camera tracking

→ animation passes

→ compositing layers

Each step adds time and complexity.

Now AI can generate editable 3D assets directly from:

→ text prompts

→ uploaded images

Which means creators start with the idea, not the pipeline.

How to attach to my 2nd tmux screen:

> tmux ls
0: 1...
items.content:
@jeremyphoward Example of how it's complicated

How to attach to my 2nd tmux screen:

> tmux ls
0: 1 windows (created Mon Mar 16 10:23:41 2026) [220x50] (attached)
1: 2 windows (created Mon Mar 16 11:45:02 2026) [220x50]
2: 1 windows (created Mon Mar 16 14:12:55 2026) [220x50]

> tmux a -t 1

Sorry it's just not user friendly

Like tmux attach 1 would be user friendly

items.title:
Accelerationist of AI

For many AI accelerationist companies, the urgent objective is to achieve sup...
items.content:
Accelerationist of AI

For many AI accelerationist companies, the urgent objective is to achieve superhuman intelligence without delay. In the early weeks and months of 2025 it often seems like nothing can truly slow the momentum of artificial intelligence. Within this landscape, a faction of "techno-optimists" in Silicon Valley is fervently accelerating progress. They are accelerationists of AI, relentlessly pushing forward on the gas pedal.

In the early months of 2025, it often seems like nothing can truly slow the momentum of artificial intelligence. Within this landscape, a faction of "techno-optimists" in Silicon Valley is fervently accelerating progress. They are known as AI accelerationists, relentlessly pushing forward on the gas pedal. SSI successfully secured $1 billion in funding from leading venture capital firms, including Andreessen Horowitz and Sequoia Capital, establishing a robust valuation of $5 billion. As of February 2025, strategic discussions are actively ongoing to raise further capital, with bold projections suggesting that SSI could be valued at an impressive $20 billion. This rapid trajectory underscores the immense confidence investors have in SSI's potential to revolutionize the industry.

OpenAI has played a pivotal role in the development of groundbreaking AI models such as GPT-4 and DALL-E. In addition to these advancements, the company is actively participating in the “Stargate” project—a significant collaboration with industry giants like Microsoft, NVIDIA, Oracle, and Softbank. This ambitious initiative aims to build a state-of-the-art supercomputer specifically designed for AI research, with investments potentially reaching up to $100 billion. This collaborative effort highlights OpenAI's commitment to driving the future of artificial intelligence at an unprecedented scale.

DeepMind has made remarkable strides in the field of artificial intelligence with groundbreaking systems like AlphaGo, which famously defeated world champion Go players, and AlphaFold, celebrated for its precision in predicting protein structures. As the company forges ahead, it remains committed to exploring innovative pathways toward achieving artificial general intelligence and superintelligence, pushing the boundaries of what AI can accomplish and revolutionizing our understanding of complex challenges in science and technology.

xAI is on a mission to develop cutting edge generative AI models with the ambitious goal of achieving superintelligence. To drive this vision forward, the company has attracted top engineers from industry leaders like Google and OpenAI, leveraging their expertise to push the boundaries of AI innovation. Additionally, xAI has secured substantial funding from investors connected to SpaceX and Tesla, further underscoring the confidence in its potential to transform the landscape of artificial intelligence.

The Artificial Superintelligence Alliance (https://t.co/lHgVTmbY7w, SingularityNET, and Ocean Protocol) aims to collaboratively develop decentralized, ethical, and safe AI systems. With a focus on advancing artificial general intelligence and artificial superintelligence, the alliance seeks to foster a robust environment for innovation while ensuring that these advancements are aligned with societal values. This collaborative effort underscores the confidence in the potential of these technologies to transform the landscape of artificial intelligence.

Anthropic has been actively engaged in research focused on AI alignment and safety, highlighting its commitment to understanding how to develop safe superintelligent systems. Their work contributes to the broader discourse on creating AI technologies that prioritize ethical considerations and align with human values, further enhancing the potential for responsible advancements in artificial intelligence.

In partnership with OpenAI, Microsoft is making significant investments in the development of superintelligent AI systems. As a key technology partner in the “Stargate” project, Microsoft is actively contributing to the creation of advanced AI supercomputers. This collaboration underscores the commitment to pushing the boundaries of AI capabilities while ensuring a focus on safety and ethical considerations in the advancement of these technologies.

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #NLProc #IoT #IIoT #PyTorch #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #Java #JavaScript #ReactJS #GoLang #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode

References

Genesis-Embodied-AI. (n.d.). Genesis [GitHub repository]. GitHub. Retrieved February 11, 2025, from https://t.co/kFc2OMdvQ4

Levin, M. (2025, February 11). Accelerationist of AI. Marketplace. Retrieved February 11, 2025, from https://t.co/9fZxoDL4xL

It's coming—RSAC 2026—world's largest & most influential annual Cybersecurity Conference. Leader @Commvault will be there showcasing the latest in ResOps (Resilience Ops), Rapid Ransomware Recovery, Agentic-powered Cyber Resilience,…
Don't miss any of it:
https://t.co/EpyEZbdOOn
...because the best time to plan recovery is before the cyber attack starts.

What you can expect from Commvault at RSAC 2026:
- Product announcements & presentations
- Live Demos
- Meet the Commvault Executives
- Public Sector Day
- The ResOps Rumble
- Rumble After-Party
- Learn how unified resilience stands up to today’s biggest threats: Misconfig Misfit, the Agentic Anomaly, and the Identity Impostor.

items.title:
Kinda, in 2014 I made $5K/mo with Nomad List, 2015 was $10K/mo with Remote OK added

Before that 200...
items.content:
Kinda, in 2014 I made $5K/mo with Nomad List, 2015 was $10K/mo with Remote OK added

Before that 2008-2013, I was making anywhere from $2K to $8K/mo with my YouTube channel

And then before 2008 I was making maybe $500 - $1K/mo with my AdSense blogs

And then around 2003 I was DJ'ing so I'd make $50 to $300 per hour to DJ, but then sometimes BBC Radio 1 would play the music I produced (PandaDNB) which would give me a royalty check of like $1000 for 6 mins!!!
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio sharing his revenue journey: from DJ'ing in 2003 to AdSense blogs, YouTube channel ($2-8K/mo), Nomad List ($5K/mo in 2014), Remote OK ($10K/mo in 2015).
items.reasoning: Interesting entrepreneurial background but not AI-specific.

items.title:
The torture of hotels touring you around gets exponentially worse the more you pay

They will litera...
items.content:
The torture of hotels touring you around gets exponentially worse the more you pay

They will literally tour you around the whole hotel with their specific opening times of every little area as if you'd ever be able to memorize that

It's completely guest hostile especially since most guests come from long flights in sweaty clothes and would love to just get to their room and lie on the bed and chill with AC on max

Every $$$ hotel check in script:

"Welcome, Mr. _____. Everything's all set for you. Here's your complimentary champagne (?????). 

My name's Sofia, and I'd love to show you around before I take you up to your room. It'll only take a few minutes, follow me!

So this is our lobby, if you ever need anything at all, but only in the day, just come find us here at reception. We're staffed from 8 to 5. Over there is our concierge desk, we can help you with restaurant reservations, taxi bookings, day trips, anything you need.

This is our lounge bar. It opens at eleven forty-five and runs until eleven fifty-two at night, later on weekends, we go until midnight on Saturdays but close at eleven thirty-seven on Sundays, just so you know. Happy hour is five twenty to six oh-five, two-for-one on cocktails. Really popular with guests. We also do a complimentary welcome drink, I'll sort that for you once we're done.

Our restaurant is just through here. Breakfast is served from six fifty-three to ten twenty-two, buffet style, included in your stay. For lunch and dinner, it's à la carte. I'd recommend booking ahead for dinner, especially on weekends, it fills up fast.

Out those doors is the pool, heated, open eight thirteen in the morning until nine forty-nine at night. Towels are provided, just show your key card. The spa is right next door, massages, facials, steam room. You can book at reception or just pop in. It's quietest before nine oh-one if you want the place to yourself.

The gym is on the second floor, open twenty-four hours except between three sixteen and three forty-four in the morning when we do a deep clean, all included. And the business center is just past the gym if you need to print anything or use a workstation.

Your room is on the fifth floor, great view from up there. The WiFi password is on the card in your welcome folder, same network throughout the whole hotel.

And here we are! So, air con controls are here, blackout curtains on this panel, minibar is stocked and refreshed daily at two oh-eight in the afternoon. Safe is in the wardrobe, instructions are inside. If you need extra pillows, towels, anything at all, just dial zero.

Is there anything I can help you with right now, or any questions?"
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio's humorous long-form post satirizing luxury hotel check-in tours with absurdly precise times, highlighting guest-hostile experiences.
items.reasoning: Entertaining but not AI-related content.

So this is our lobby, if you ever need anything at all, but only in the day, just come find us here at reception. We're staffed from 8 to 5. Over there is our concierge desk, we can help you with restaurant reservations, taxi bookings, day trips, anything you need.

It opens at eleven forty-five and runs until eleven fifty-two at night, later on weekends, we go until midnight on Saturdays but close at eleven thirty-seven on Sundays, just so you know. items.content: @TMobileBusiness Congratulations, recognition like this reflects how network capabilities translate into dependable performance under pressure. @TMobileBusiness Well-framed message, because it shifts the discussion from raw speed to operational continuity.

T-Mobile points to a more concrete reality since security and coverage limits emerge exactly when business is most exposed. Solutions like SuperMobile make sense in this context, where adaptive performance and seamless coverage support consistent execution in the field.

items.title:
What if a single text prompt could generate an entire animated 3D scene?

Not a concept.

A real sce...
items.content:
What if a single text prompt could generate an entire animated 3D scene?

Not a concept.

A real scene you can edit and export into production tools.

AI is starting to compress what used to be an entire VFX pipeline into one step.

I recently explored how this works with @autodesk.

And the implications for creative production are huge.

Let’s break it down.

items.title: For the first time in my life, I went to an all-inclusive resort. I have been of the opinion that if...
items.content:
For the first time in my life, I went to an all-inclusive resort. I have been of the opinion that if you pay for the service upfront, you will not get anything good, but everyone told me that those all-inclusive resorts are pure paradises.

So, I paid for a 5-star resort in the Dominican Republic, in Punta Cana.

And I was right all this time.

Rude, unmotivated personnel. Working slowly or not working at all.

If you haven't finished your breakfast before the official breakfast restaurant closure, they will ask you to leave, and if you continue eating your breakfast, they would start cleaning your table right around your plates and glasses.

On the bright side: the ocean and the sand are nice.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov shares a negative experience at a Dominican Republic all-inclusive resort (off-topic).
items.reasoning: Entirely off-topic. No AI relevance.
items.summary_html: Burkov shares a negative experience at a Dominican Republic all-inclusive resort (off-topic).

items.title: 96 - Jesus Story English Coloring Set Book - Help Former Indian Slave Children Experience Jesus's Lo...
items.content:
96 - Jesus Story English Coloring Set Book - Help Former Indian Slave Children Experience Jesus's Love! 

In numerous regions of India, countless children face the harsh realities of the caste system. Those born into the lowest caste often endure severe abuse, neglect, and even enslavement by individuals from higher castes. In response to this injustice, RevelationMedia is teaming up with Set Free Alliance to introduce the hope of Jesus to children who have been rescued from slavery, particularly in areas where Telugu is predominantly spoken.

These children receive comprehensive support that includes education, safe housing, counseling, and Biblical teachings provided by the dedicated team of local Christians from Set Free Alliance. Additionally, thanks to generous donors like you, each child is gifted their own set of The Real Story of Jesus coloring books, allowing them to engage with the message of hope and love.

That's where you can make a difference. Your donation to RevelationMedia will directly fund the printing of additional sets of The Real Story of Jesus, ensuring that they reach the hands of children in India who have never before encountered the profound message of Jesus' love for them.

When you choose to donate to help children in India experience Jesus’ love, we want to show our gratitude by sending you your very own set of English coloring books as a thank-you gift. The Real Story of Jesus storybook measures 6″ x 6″, contains 96 pages, and is beautifully illustrated in full color. It references over 300 Bible verses and covers the story from creation to the second coming of Christ.

Alongside this book serves as the perfect interactive companion to the storybook. For a limited time, it also includes a pack of colored pencils, so you can enjoy creating and exploring the story in a fun and colorful way!

Sending “The Real Story of Jesus” book to former slave children or rescued children in India may be seen as more impactful than clothing and toys for several reasons: The book offers spiritual guidance and hope, helping children understand their worth and the love that Jesus has for them. This message can foster a sense of dignity and purpose that material goods alone cannot provide.

While clothing and toys provide some relief and enjoyment, a storybook filled with biblical teachings can have a lasting influence on a child’s values, beliefs, and understanding of their identity. The book serves as a tool for learning, not just about spiritual themes but also about storytelling and comprehension. It can spark curiosity and encourage the children to read more, aiding in their overall education. The accompanying coloring book allows children to engage creatively with the material. This form of interaction can enhance their understanding of the stories and concepts, making learning both enjoyable and memorable.

Sharing the message of Jesus through the book can foster a sense of community as children come together to discuss the stories and lessons learned. It encourages connection and support among them. The stories in the book can provide comfort and hope amid their challenging circumstances. They can see that even in difficult situations, there is love and purpose, helping them envision a brighter future.

Understanding biblical stories empowers children by giving them moral frameworks and encouraging them to make positive choices, which can be particularly valuable for those coming from backgrounds of adversity. By focusing on spiritual and emotional support through “The Real Story of Jesus, we can help these children heal and grow beyond their immediate circumstances, setting a foundation for a hopeful future.

Books ensure the intended message or resource is delivered directly to the children, whereas money might not always be used as intended due to logistical or administrative challenges. The book provides emotional, spiritual, and intellectual nourishment, which money cannot directly offer.

items.title:
Grand Passage: Exploring LA’s Subterranean Realm

Grand Tunnel, is a historic tunnel in Los Angeles....
items.content:
Grand Passage: Exploring LA’s Subterranean Realm

Grand Tunnel, is a historic tunnel in Los Angeles. It was a passageway for streetcars from a century until the 1950. The tunnel runs underneath Bunker Hill, connecting the Civic Center.

In its early years, the tunnel was considered a major engineering architectural feat, as it was one of the few tunnels in the United States that was built specifically for streetcars. It was also the tunnel in Los Angeles to be electrified.

Over the years, the tunnel has undergone several renovations and upgrades to accommodate changing transportation needs. Today, it remains an important link between downtown Los Angeles and the surrounding areas.

The Grand Tunnel is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, and it continues to be an important landmark in the city's history.

Grand Tunnel, Los Angeles. Grand Tunnel cannot not found be on GPS or map. (Google Maps or Apple Maps). Grand Tunnel address cannot be found on search engine Google.
